relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Eliphaz challenged Job to seek an answer among the holy ones.
He warned that vexation and jealousy destroy the foolish.
He claimed to have seen the fool take root and then collapse.
The fool’s children, he said, end far from safety.
The hungry consume the fool’s harvest.
Trouble, to Eliphaz, does not simply sprout from nowhere.
Yet humanity is born into trouble like sparks rising upward.
He would seek the Divine and set his case there.
The Divine does wonders beyond counting.
Rain and water are sent upon earth and fields.
The lowly are lifted and mourners brought to safety.
Crafty schemes are frustrated.
The wise are caught in their own craftiness.
Those who trusted daylight grope in darkness.
The needy are saved from violent mouths and strong hands.
Hope opens for the poor, and injustice is silenced.
Eliphaz calls suffering divine discipline.
He speaks of the One who wounds and heals.
He promises rescue through six and seven troubles.
Famine and war, he says, will not finally claim the corrected one.
Even the lash of the tongue will not destroy.
Devastation and wildness become laughable.
Field stones and wild animals become covenantal peace.
The tent becomes peace, the dwelling whole.
Seed multiplies like grass.
Death comes in season, like gathered grain.
Eliphaz closes with certainty: we have searched this out, so hear it.
